caveats: a: No published six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix was located for 541890.
a: The 541800 mix includes advertising and public-relations businesses that are more desk-intensive than much of the retained physical-service work.
a: Exposure describes task substitution or avoidable hiring, not immediately realized savings.
rho: There is no observed 541890 implementation rate.
rho: The range assumes use of commercially available tools and does not assume a proprietary data advantage.

s5: A qualifying transfer excludes closures and internal reorganizations, which the survey does not cleanly separate.

q: Retention can differ sharply between a retainer-led demonstration provider and a transaction-led promotional-products distributor.

d5: The code's component activities may have materially different demand trajectories.

o: The value is lower for digital-first promotional programs and higher for field demonstrations and installed physical graphics.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business customers buy recurring campaign, promotional-program, demonstration, or display-support work under repeat orders, projects, retainers, or account arrangements; merchandise and fabrication can be pass-through components.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence because the code spans advertising-specialty coordination, public merchandise demonstrations, and display lettering. The lens retains only repeat outsourced client-service relationships and excludes project-only fabrication and pure resale.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off sign fabrication or installation projects, pure merchandise resale without an outsourced service relationship, captive marketing teams, internal units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market external providers of recurring or repeat promotional-product coordination, product demonstrations, and display-lettering services, where the provider remains accountable for client service, creative or campaign coordination, and delivery.

caveats: No published six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix was located for 541890.
The 541800 mix includes advertising and public-relations businesses that are more desk-intensive than much of the retained physical-service work.
Exposure describes task substitution or avoidable hiring, not immediately realized savings.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-and-time study across promotional-product distributors, demonstration agencies, and display-lettering firms that maps hours and wages to artwork, sales support, order administration, sourcing, field delivery, and installation; paired before-and-after AI workflow adoption data.
a.rationale: The broad 541800 wage mix contains 16.77% management employment and multiple business, arts/design, sales, office, production, and transport groups (S2). In the retained activities, AI can reduce drafting, artwork variants, proofing, proposal and outreach preparation, order-status responses, and reporting. In-person demonstrations, client persuasion, vendor exception handling, physical lettering, and delivery still require people, so less than half of current wage work is treated as exposed.

caveats: Occupation projections are not constant-price, constant-quality demand measures for 541890 and are used only as directional context.
The code's component activities may have materially different demand trajectories.
d5.change_evidence: Industry revenue volume data split among promotional products, demonstrations, and display lettering; recurring-order cohorts; and customer marketing-budget allocations between physical, experiential, and digital channels.
d5.rationale: Demand for promotion and physical display support can persist where clients need tangible merchandise, field demonstrations, or site-specific graphics, but the current basket faces substitution toward digital channels and self-service tools. Adjacent occupation projections are mixed to negative: advertising sales agents are projected to decline 6%, advertising and promotions managers decline 2%, and graphic designers grow 2% from 2024 to 2034 (S3, S4, S5). This supports a roughly flat central quantity outlook rather than a growth assumption.

caveats: NAICS descriptions identify activities, not the share of firms or revenue in each business model.
Eligibility is assessed at the firm level and may differ from establishment coding.
e.change_evidence: A Census microdata tabulation or a commercial establishment database matched to websites and revenue descriptions, classifying 541890 LMM firms by repeat service revenue, project-only fabrication, and pure resale.
e.rationale: The code explicitly includes advertising-specialty coordination, merchandise demonstrations, and display lettering (S1). The lens excludes the project-only fabrication and pure-resale portion, while repeat client programs in each listed activity can satisfy the outsourced-service requirement. The resulting eligible share is judged to be near one-half rather than universal.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541890 ($4,995,994,780) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541890 ($19,434,299,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $189,523 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541890); class <5: 4,615 firms, avg revenue/firm $481,015 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $75,279 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 806 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,794,295 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $280,807 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 442 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,905,484 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $611,208 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 398 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,154,884 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,902,239 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 84 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,233,821 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,296,593 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $112,013,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,530,113 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 482

caveats: This is a judgment about the retained lens, not an observed industry survival share.
The value is lower for digital-first promotional programs and higher for field demonstrations and installed physical graphics.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and churn data identifying whether promotional-product ordering, demonstrations, and display graphics were eliminated, self-served, or moved to software or internal teams; vendor records for field-service utilization.
o.rationale: The retained service basket includes physical activities: coordinating delivery of branded specialties, demonstrating merchandise in public places, and applying graphics or lettering to signs (S1). Those outputs require accountable vendors even if planning and administration become more automated. The deduction from one reflects digital promotion, self-service creative tools, and customer in-housing that can eliminate some outsourced coordination.

caveats: No industry-specific evidence was found for the portion of automation benefits passed through to customers.
Retention can differ sharply between a retainer-led demonstration provider and a transaction-led promotional-products distributor.
q.change_evidence: Client contracts and renewal cohorts showing billing basis, pass-through clauses, labor-versus-merchandise gross margin, competitive rebids, and price changes after workflow automation.
q.rationale: Repeat account work and fixed-price project components permit an operator to retain some lower drafting and coordination cost before repricing. However, the service is closely tied to customer marketing budgets and often bundles physical merchandise or fabrication, making labor savings visible and contestable at renewal. BLS notes managers negotiate advertising contracts with clients and partners (S3), consistent with customer sharing and price competition reducing retention.

caveats: There is no observed 541890 implementation rate.
The range assumes use of commercially available tools and does not assume a proprietary data advantage.
rho.change_evidence: Workflow-level adoption and quality-control records from comparable firms, including AI-assisted proofing error rates, client approval-cycle time, rework rates, and labor hours per order or campaign.
rho.rationale: Drafting and administrative uses can be embedded in ordinary account and production workflows, but client approvals, brand accuracy, product specifications, vendor data, and field execution limit operational implementation. BLS describes advertising and promotions work as involving budgets, contracts, campaigns, pricing, client meetings, and direction of staff (S3), supporting a material but incomplete five-year implementation share.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year stated owner plans across U.S. businesses whose owners primarily work in the business, not realized qualifying control transfers in 541890 LMM firms. Its 14% all-owner sell, go-public, or transfer response is used as the central cross-industry starting point; the 17% response among owners aged 55 or older informs the upper range. The proxy likely overstates completed qualifying transfers because plans may not close and includes gifts or rare public offerings, but may understate buyer-led sales not planned by respondents.
caveats: The source is a 2024 survey, not an industry transaction census.
A qualifying transfer excludes closures and internal reorganizations, which the survey does not cleanly separate.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed lower-middle-market transaction data for promotional-products, experiential-demonstration, and sign/display-service providers, plus owner-age and succession-plan data for those firms.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 14% of surveyed primary-work business owners planned to sell, go public, or transfer in the next five years; among owners 55 or older, the figure was 17% (S6). The base discounts stated intent for completion, non-control transfers, and the service-specific population mismatch.

principal_driver: Repeat client programs can pair AI-assisted coordination with physical delivery that still requires an accountable operator.
principal_weakness: The six-digit code is heterogeneous, and the most relevant transfer and labor evidence is not measured for its retained lower-middle-market service subset.
